title: Subword Complexes in Coxeter Groups
overall_summary: The paper studies subword complexes Δ(Q,π) attached to a word Q in the simple reflections of a Coxeter system and an element π of the Coxeter group. Its central topological result is that every subword complex is vertex-decomposable and hence shellable, and in fact is homeomorphic to a ball or a sphere. The proof combines a deletion-link recursion on the first letter of Q, the Coxeter exchange axiom, and the Demazure product, which detects when an arbitrary word contains a reduced expression for a given element. For the original problem, the most relevant transferable pattern is: encode choices of reductions or resolutions as a combinatorial complex, prove a recursive shelling, control codimension-one incidences by an exchange-type lemma, and then conclude ball/contractibility once the sphere case is excluded.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 2.1 and Lemma 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (Π,Σ) be a Coxeter system with Coxeter length ℓ, let Q=(σ1,...,σm) be an ordered word in simple reflections σi∈Σ, and let π∈Π. A subword P⊆Q means an ordered subsequence with its positions remembered. A subword P represents π if its ordered product is a reduced expression for π, and P contains π if some subword of P represents π. The subword complex Δ(Q,π) is the simplicial complex whose faces are complements Q∖P such that P contains π. Lemma 2.2 says that Δ(Q,π) is pure and that its facets are exactly the complements Q∖P where P⊆Q represents π. Consequently every facet has m−ℓ(π) vertices, so Δ(Q,π) has dimension m−ℓ(π)−1 when nonvoid.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If Q∖P is a face, then P contains a subword D representing π. Since D⊆P, the complement Q∖D contains Q∖P as a larger face. Thus every face lies in a face whose complement is a reduced expression for π. Conversely, if P itself represents π, then no proper subword of P can still contain π, because all reduced expressions for π have length ℓ(π). Hence Q∖P is maximal among faces. This identifies the facets and proves purity, since all complements of reduced expressions for π have the same cardinality m−ℓ(π).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every Coxeter system (Π,Σ), every word Q in Σ, and every π∈Π, the subword complex Δ(Q,π) is vertex-decomposable; therefore it is shellable. Here a pure simplicial complex is vertex-decomposable if it is {∅} or if it has a vertex v such that both the deletion del(v,Δ), consisting of faces not containing v, and the link link(v,Δ), consisting of faces G disjoint from v with G∪{v}∈Δ, are vertex-decomposable. The implication vertex-decomposable implies shellable is the standard Provan-Billera result used by the paper.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Induct on the length of the word Q. Write Q=(σ,σ2,...,σm)=(σ,Q′), where σ is the first simple reflection and Q′ is the remaining word. Consider the vertex of Δ(Q,π) corresponding to the first position σ. The link of this vertex consists of faces G supported on Q′ such that G∪{σ} is a face of Δ(Q,π). In complement language this means that Q′∖G contains π, so the link is naturally Δ(Q′,π). For the deletion, a face G supported on Q′ lies in del(σ,Δ(Q,π)) precisely when the complement σ together with Q′∖G contains π. There are two cases. If ℓ(σπ)>ℓ(π), then no reduced expression for π can begin with σ, so any reduced subword witnessing containment of π avoids the first σ; hence the deletion equals Δ(Q′,π), the same as the link. If ℓ(σπ)<ℓ(π), then using the first σ to build π is equivalent to having the tail contain σπ, and the deletion is Δ(Q′,σπ). In both cases the link and deletion are subword complexes on the shorter word Q′, so the induction applies.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 3.1 and Lemma 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (Π,Σ) be a Coxeter system. For a word P=(σ1,...,σm) in Σ, its Demazure product δ(P) is defined by the multiplication rule eτ eσ = eτσ if ℓ(τσ)>ℓ(τ), and eτ eσ = eτ if ℓ(τσ)<ℓ(τ), for τ∈Π and σ∈Σ; δ(P) is the element satisfying eσ1...eσm=eδ(P). Let ≥ denote Bruhat order, where τ≥π means that a reduced word for τ contains a reduced word for π. Lemma 3.4 states: (1) δ(P)≥π if and only if P contains π. (2) If δ(P)=π, then every subword P′⊆P that contains π has δ(P′)=π. (3) If δ(P)>π, then P contains a subword T representing some element τ with τ>π and |T|=ℓ(τ)=ℓ(π)+1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For part (1), first suppose δ(P)=π′≥π. Scan P from left to right and omit exactly those reflections that do not increase length in the Demazure multiplication. The retained subword is a reduced expression for π′. Since π′≥π in Bruhat order, this reduced expression contains a reduced expression for π, so P contains π. Conversely, suppose a subword T⊆P represents π. The proof is by induction on |P|. Let σ be the last reflection of P. By definition of Demazure product, δ(P∖σ) is either δ(P) or δ(P)σ, and δ(P)σ<δ(P). If πσ>π, then a reduced expression for π cannot end by using the last σ, so T⊆P∖σ, and induction gives π≤δ(P∖σ)≤δ(P). If πσ<π, then a subword T′ of T represents πσ, and T′⊆P∖σ; induction gives πσ≤δ(P∖σ), and the Bruhat compatibility with multiplying by σ yields π≤δ(P). For part (2), if P′⊆P contains π, then by part (1) δ(P′)≥π; also P contains δ(P′), so part (1) gives δ(P)≥δ(P′). Since δ(P)=π, this forces δ(P′)=π. For part (3), choose an element τ on a saturated Bruhat chain from π to δ(P), so π<τ≤δ(P) and ℓ(τ)=ℓ(π)+1. By part (1), P contains a reduced expression T for τ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let T be a word in the simple reflections of a Coxeter system, and let π be a Coxeter group element such that |T|=ℓ(π)+1. Then: (1) there are at most two positions σ in T such that T∖σ represents π; (2) if δ(T)=π, then there are two distinct positions σ in T such that T∖σ represents π; and (3) if T itself is a reduced expression representing an element τ with τ>π, then there is exactly one position σ in T such that T∖σ represents π.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For part (1), assume toward contradiction that three positions σ1,σ2,σ3 in increasing order can each be omitted to obtain a reduced expression for π. Write T=T1 σ1 T2 σ2 T3 σ3 T4. Equality of the two products obtained by omitting σ1 and σ2 gives T1 T2 σ2 T3 σ3 T4 = T1 σ1 T2 T3 σ3 T4 in the group. Cancelling the common left factor T1 and right factor T3 σ3 T4 gives T2 σ2 = σ1 T2. Then the product T1 σ1 T2 σ2 T3 T4 equals T1 σ1 σ1 T2 T3 T4 = T1 T2 T3 T4. This expresses π by a word of length |T|−2, contradicting ℓ(π)=|T|−1. For part (2), δ(T)=π and |T|=ℓ(π)+1 imply that in the Demazure scan exactly one reflection σ is skipped because it decreases length, and omitting this σ gives a reduced expression for π. More explicitly, write T=T1 σ T2, where T1T2 represents π and, if T1 represents τ1, then τ1σ<τ1. By the Coxeter exchange axiom, omitting some reflection σ′ from T1 gives a reduced expression for τ1σ. It follows that both T∖σ and T∖σ′ represent π, giving two distinct omissions. Part (3) is the Coxeter exchange condition in the case of a reduced word T for τ and a Bruhat-covering subelement π of length one less.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Δ be a shellable simplicial complex such that every codimension-one face of Δ is contained in at most two facets. Then Δ is a topological manifold with boundary and is homeomorphic to either a ball or a sphere. Moreover, the facets of the topological boundary of Δ are exactly the codimension-one faces of Δ that are contained in exactly one facet of Δ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Björner-Las Vergnas-Sturmfels-White-Ziegler, Oriented Matroids, Proposition 4.7.22. The invoked result says that a shellable pure complex with no codimension-one face contained in more than two facets is a shellable pseudomanifold with possible boundary, and shellability forces such a complex to have the topology of a ball when the boundary is nonempty and of a sphere when the boundary is empty. The description of boundary facets follows from the same proposition: in a manifold-with-boundary triangulation, the boundary ridges are precisely those incident to only one top-dimensional facet.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (Π,Σ) be a Coxeter system, let Q be a word in Σ, and let π∈Π. The subword complex Δ(Q,π) is either a ball or a sphere. More precisely, for any face Q∖P of Δ(Q,π), meaning P⊆Q contains π, the face Q∖P lies in the topological boundary of Δ(Q,π) if and only if the Demazure product δ(P) is not equal to π.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First consider a codimension-one face Q∖T of Δ(Q,π). Since facets are complements of reduced expressions for π, such a T has length ℓ(π)+1 and contains π. The facets containing Q∖T correspond exactly to positions σ in T such that T∖σ represents π. Lemma 3.5(1) says there are at most two such positions. Since Δ(Q,π) is shellable by Theorem 2.5, Lemma 3.6 applies and gives the ball-or-sphere conclusion. For the boundary criterion, let Q∖P be a face. If δ(P)≠π, then because P contains π, Lemma 3.4 gives δ(P)>π. Lemma 3.4(3) provides a subword T⊆P representing an element τ>π with |T|=ℓ(π)+1. Then Q∖T is a codimension-one face containing Q∖P. Since T is reduced for τ>π, Lemma 3.5(3) says Q∖T lies in exactly one facet, hence it is a boundary ridge by Lemma 3.6; therefore its subface Q∖P is also in the boundary. Conversely, suppose δ(P)=π. Let Q∖T be any codimension-one face containing Q∖P. Then T⊆P, T contains π, and |T|=ℓ(π)+1. Lemma 3.4(2) gives δ(T)=π, and Lemma 3.5(2) says Q∖T is contained in two facets. By Lemma 3.6 all such codimension-one faces are interior ridges, and hence Q∖P is an interior face rather than a boundary face.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a Coxeter system (Π,Σ), a word Q in Σ, and π∈Π, the subword complex Δ(Q,π) is a sphere if δ(Q)=π and is a ball otherwise.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Theorem 3.7 to the empty face, whose complement is the whole word Q. If δ(Q)=π, then the empty face is not in the boundary, so the boundary is empty and the ball-or-sphere complex must be a sphere. If δ(Q)≠π, then the empty face lies in the boundary, so the boundary is nonempty and Lemma 3.6 gives that the complex is a ball.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Topological consequence used in Lemma 4.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Δ=Δ(Q,π) be a subword complex and let F=Q∖P be a face. Identify the vertices not in F with the positions of P. Then link(F,Δ) is naturally the subword complex Δ(P,π). Therefore, if ℓ=ℓ(π), the link of F is a sphere of dimension |P|−ℓ−1 when δ(P)=π, and is contractible, indeed a ball, when δ(P)≠π.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A face H lies in link(Q∖P,Δ) exactly when H is disjoint from Q∖P, so H⊆P, and (Q∖P)∪H is a face of Δ(Q,π). The complement of (Q∖P)∪H inside Q is P∖H, so this condition says exactly that P∖H contains π. This is precisely the definition of H being a face of Δ(P,π), after viewing H as the complement in P of P∖H. Now apply Corollary 3.8 to the word P: if δ(P)=π the link is spherical of dimension |P|−ℓ(π)−1; if F is a face and δ(P)≠π, then δ(P)>π by Lemma 3.4, so Corollary 3.8 says Δ(P,π) is a ball, hence contractible.

proof_techniques.name: Complement-of-certificates complex
proof_techniques.description: The paper encodes reduced expressions indirectly: facets of Δ(Q,π) are not the reduced subwords P themselves, but their complements Q∖P. More generally, a face is the complement of any subword P that contains some reduced expression for π. This reversal of containment makes the complex pure automatically, because all minimal certificates for containing π have the same size ℓ(π), and it turns enlargement of a face into deletion of unnecessary letters from a certificate.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, one possible route is to encode a matching or reduction certificate by the complement of the data that certifies reducibility, so that maximal cells or facets correspond to minimal reduction patterns. This may turn the topology of matchings into a shellable complex controlled by minimal cancellation certificates.

proof_techniques.name: Deletion-link recursion on the first generator
proof_techniques.description: The vertex-decomposition proof always peels off the first letter σ of the word Q. The link of that vertex is Δ(Q′,π), where Q′ is Q with σ removed. The deletion is either the same complex Δ(Q′,π), when σ cannot start a reduced expression for π, or Δ(Q′,σπ), when σ is a left descent of π. Thus both local pieces are subword complexes of strictly shorter words, giving a clean induction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A comparable induction for F_w would peel off a distinguished local feature, such as the first boundary point or a forced inverse match, and identify the link and deletion or analogous local subcomplexes with the same construction for shorter words. The quasi-reduced hypothesis may be useful precisely for making such local cases well behaved.

proof_techniques.name: Demazure product as a monotone closure invariant
proof_techniques.description: The Demazure product δ(P) is a length-nonincreasing modification of the usual product: when multiplying by a simple reflection that would decrease Coxeter length, the product stays fixed. Lemma 3.4 shows that this invariant exactly detects containment: δ(P)≥π in Bruhat order if and only if P contains a reduced expression for π. It also distinguishes interior from boundary data: δ(P)=π means no excess beyond π, while δ(P)>π means P contains a one-step larger reduced subword.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For free-group matching complexes, an analogous closure or normal-form invariant could track whether a partial configuration still certifies that the word reduces to the identity and whether it contains excess reducibility. Such an invariant could replace ad hoc cancellation arguments with a boundary criterion.

proof_techniques.name: Exchange axiom controls codimension-one incidence
proof_techniques.description: The proof of the ball-or-sphere theorem reduces codimension-one topology to a length ℓ(π)+1 word T. Facets containing the ridge Q∖T correspond to one-letter deletions T∖σ that represent π. Lemma 3.5, derived from the Coxeter exchange axiom, says there are at most two such deletions; there are two in the Demazure-product-equal case and exactly one when T is reduced for a Bruhat-larger element. This is the local combinatorial fact that makes the complex a pseudomanifold with boundary.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, the analogous target would be a local exchange lemma for matchings or cancellations: a codimension-one partial configuration should have at most two completions/resolutions, with a criterion for when it has one versus two. Proving such a lemma would make the global topological argument much closer to the subword-complex proof.

proof_techniques.name: Shellable pseudomanifold implies ball or sphere
proof_techniques.description: After shellability is established, the paper does not compute homotopy groups directly. Instead it verifies that each codimension-one face is in at most two facets, invokes the standard shellable pseudomanifold criterion, and obtains that the whole complex is homeomorphic to a ball or sphere. The boundary is then identified combinatorially as the set of ridges incident to only one facet.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a barycentric subdivision or face poset model of F_w can be shown shellable and to have controlled codimension-one incidences, contractibility would follow once one proves that the spherical case cannot occur or that the resulting object has nonempty boundary.

proof_techniques.name: Boundary and links from smaller ambient words
proof_techniques.description: For any face Q∖P of a subword complex, its link is naturally Δ(P,π), the same construction with the ambient word replaced by the complement P. Corollary 3.8 then classifies every link as a sphere or ball according to δ(P)=π or δ(P)≠π. This gives a local-to-global mechanism: the same invariant that classifies the whole complex also classifies all links.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, it would be useful to show that the link of a cell is again a matching complex for a smaller or residual word. Then an inductive classification of links as contractible or spherical could support a proof that the whole CW complex is a ball or otherwise contractible.

proof_techniques.name: Canonical simplification and acyclic facet orientation
proof_techniques.description: In Section 4 and Remark 4.5, the paper considers simplifying a subword P to the lexicographically first reduced subword D with the same Demazure product. Across an interior ridge, the facet adjacency graph is oriented toward the facet determined by this simplification. The paper notes that this directed graph is acyclic because the ridge relation is a subrelation of lexicographic order.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, a canonical way to simplify a crossing matching to a preferred resolved or reduced matching could orient the adjacency graph of cells and potentially yield a discrete Morse function or shelling order.

other_useful_info: The paper uses the convention that {∅} is a sphere of dimension −1 with reduced homology Z in dimension −1; this matters for base cases where the ambient word itself is a reduced expression. The introduction points to Björner-Wachs on shellability of Bruhat intervals and Björner on weak-order intervals being homotopy equivalent to balls or spheres as related Coxeter-theoretic topology. The paper cites Provan-Billera for vertex-decomposable implies shellable and Björner-Las Vergnas-Sturmfels-White-Ziegler, Oriented Matroids, Proposition 4.7.22, for the shellable pseudomanifold ball-or-sphere criterion. Remark 4.5 states that the lexicographic Demazure-simplification orientation of the facet adjacency graph is acyclic, but the stronger claim that every linear extension gives a shelling is presented only as a belief, not as a proved theorem.
why_relevant: Subword complexes are shellable balls or spheres built from choices of resolving/deleting letters in words. Since uncrossing posets are related to Bruhat order, these results may provide an alternative shellability or contractibility framework for F_w.
